R04--support--R02: R02 demands that 'all code should be robust and validated' but, as the judgment notes, leaves 'robust' and 'validated' undefined; R04's process directive to 'build, test, and validate incrementally' supplies the concrete working method that makes validation actually happen, so following R04 is what makes R02 achievable in practice.
R05--refinement--R04: R04 prescribes 'build, test, and validate incrementally'; R05's 'never make large untested changes' restates that same norm as a sharper prohibition — a large untested change is precisely the violation of incremental development, so R05 carves the broad process rule into a concrete forbidden case.
R06--support--R03: R03 requires 'MS2.0 design with clear module boundaries and interfaces' but does not say where those patterns come from; R06's duty to 'always consult MULTI_ROW_REFERENCE.md and AMD_GEMM_REFERENCE.md for architectural patterns' makes R03 easier to satisfy by pointing to the documents that contain the sanctioned boundary and interface patterns.
R06--duplication--R26: Both rules state the consult-the-docs norm: R06 says 'always consult MULTI_ROW_REFERENCE.md and AMD_GEMM_REFERENCE.md' and R26 says 'reference documentation - consult frequently'. R26 adds nothing beyond R06 except dropping the file names, so the second statement is the same obligation written twice; arguably R06 is the more specific of the pair, but neither gates or extends the other.
R07--support--R03: R03's 'clear module boundaries and interfaces' is abstract until R07 supplies the actual interface mechanisms: 'ready-valid handshaking, FIFO decoupling, and coding conventions from AMD reference'. Adopting these conventions is what gives R03's boundaries a concrete, implementable form, so R07 makes R03 followable.
R10--checkpoint--R02: R10 ('after any change, run test_registers to verify device health') imposes no new code-quality obligation; it designates a recurring moment to verify that R02's demand for 'robust and validated' code still holds on the device. It is purely a verification duty that enforces R02 rather than extending it.
R12--support--R11: R11 forbids hardcoded results ('No Hardcoded Results') but does not say what to do instead; R12's 'all tests must use golden references or computed values' provides the positive method — if every expected value is a golden reference or computed, none can be hardcoded — so R12 is exactly how an agent satisfies R11.
R14--refinement--R13: R13 prescribes the build command 'use `make clean && make`'; R14's 'never skip clean' restates the same norm as a prohibition targeting the one tempting shortcut — building without the clean step. It narrows R13 to its critical component rather than adding a new obligation, making it a prohibition-twin refinement.
R15--support--R18: R18's principle 'start with basic health checks, build complexity' is made operational by R15, which names the concrete starting point: 'run test suite starting with `test_registers`'. R15 tells the agent exactly which basic health check comes first, making R18 easy to follow. One could also argue this is a refinement (R15 concretizes R18); the support reading emphasizes that R15 is the enabling instance.
R16--duplication--R22: R16 says 'update README.md for technical changes' and R22 says 'for technical details, specifications, and implementation notes, update README.md instead' — both route technical content to README.md. R22 adds the contrast with CLAUDE.md, but the core obligation (technical detail goes in README) is written twice, making the pair an ineffective restatement rather than a refinement.
R18--refinement--R04: R04's general process rule 'build, test, and validate incrementally' is narrowed by R18 to the testing phase: 'start with basic health checks, build complexity' is the incremental principle applied specifically to how tests are ordered, so R18 restates R04's norm in a more concrete, domain-specific form.
R20--support--R19: R19's terse 'Never assume' gives no procedure; R20's 'always verify against references' supplies the positive action that prevents assuming — checking results against a reference is precisely how an agent replaces assumption with evidence, so R20 makes R19 satisfiable in practice.
R22--support--R21: R21 restricts CLAUDE.md edits to 'major architectural shifts, philosophy/workflow changes, new integration points, or critical operational procedure changes'; R22 makes that restriction livable by routing everything else: 'for technical details, specifications, and implementation notes, update README.md instead'. Having a sanctioned destination for detail removes the pressure to violate R21.
R23--refinement--R03: R03's broad 'MS2.0 design with clear module boundaries and interfaces' is restated concretely by R23 for a specific scope: 'core RTL modules - keep modular and parameterized'. R23 names the artifacts (core RTL modules) and adds the parameterization requirement, narrowing the abstract modularity norm to a checkable instance.
R27--refinement--R07: R07 says to 'adopt ready-valid handshaking' from the AMD reference; R27 turns that adoption directive into a universal protocol requirement: 'all inter-module communication uses ready/valid protocol (ready/valid signals between all FSMs)'. The quantifier 'all' and the FSM-level scope make R27 the concrete, enforceable restatement of R07's handshaking clause.
R28--refinement--R07: R07's clause to adopt 'FIFO decoupling' is concretized by R28, which specifies where and why: 'FIFOs between pipeline stages for flow control and timing isolation'. R28 narrows the general convention to a specific structural placement, so it refines the FIFO portion of R07.
R29--support--R28: The relation here runs through enablement: R28's 'FIFOs between pipeline stages for flow control' is the mechanism that makes R29's prohibition 'No Blocking Operations' achievable — FIFO buffering is what lets stages proceed without blocking each other. Note the edge direction (R29 -> R28) is debatable: the judgment says non-blocking flow 'is what FIFOs buy', which reads more naturally as R28 supporting R29; as drawn, the rationale is that the no-blocking demand motivates inserting FIFOs between stages.
R33--refinement--R03: R03's generic 'clear module boundaries' is given a specific architectural cut by R33: 'clear separation of control and data paths'. Separating control from data is one concrete way the module boundaries must be drawn, so R33 restates the modularity norm at a narrower, checkable level.
C01--support_(defines)--R30: C01 defines the unit being partitioned: 'Native Vector (NV): a vector of GFP numbers, may contain multiple groups'. R30's 'automatic partitioning across rows (V) and columns (C)' distributes NVs (V and C are NV counts per the distribution formulas), so the rule is unintelligible without this definition.
C01--support_(defines)--R01: R01 orders the agent to 'read the Critical Terminology section at the beginning of every conversation'; C01's NV definition is one of the terminology entries that section consists of. Per R01's judgment, the rule 'has no content without' the terminology nodes — C01 supplies part of the very content R01 mandates reading.
C02--support_(defines)--R30: C02 defines the row dimension of the partition target: 'Row: a row of compute tiles, each with its own row_bram for activations... row_bram holds the left matrix'. R30's 'automatic partitioning across rows (V)' only makes sense once 'row' and its per-row storage are defined by C02.
C02--support_(defines)--R23: R23 requires 'core RTL modules - keep modular and parameterized'; C02's description of rows of compute tiles 'each with its own row_bram (mapped to a GDDR6 channel)' identifies the repeated, per-row structure that modularity and parameterization (e.g., number of rows, BRAM mapping) must capture, defining what the core RTL modules actually are.
C03--support_(defines)--R33: R33 demands 'clear separation of control and data paths'; C03 defines the physical substrate of that separation: 'BRAM for low-latency result storage and command buffering; GDDR6 for high-bandwidth matrix data'. The command/control side (BRAM) versus bulk data side (GDDR6) is the architectural fact that gives R33's separation its concrete meaning.
C03--support_(defines)--R03: R03's 'clear module boundaries and interfaces' presupposes knowing where the natural boundaries lie; C03's dual-memory split (low-latency BRAM versus high-bandwidth GDDR6) defines one of the fundamental architectural seams along which MS2.0 module boundaries are drawn. This edge is weaker than C03->R33 since R03 is generic, but the definition still grounds the boundary structure R03 refers to.
C04--support_(defines)--R30: C04 defines the column/tile dimension of partitioning: a '16x16 compute tile' array where 'mlp_bram holds shared weights (right matrix) broadcast to all columns'. R30's 'automatic partitioning across... columns (C)' is only applicable once C04 establishes what columns are and how weights are distributed to them.
C04--support_(defines)--R23: R23's 'core RTL modules - keep modular and parameterized' is grounded by C04's structural fact of a '16x16 compute tile' array with shared mlp_bram weight storage: the regular 2-D tile grid is exactly the structure whose dimensions should be parameters and whose tiles should be modular units.
C05--support_(defines)--R30: C05 gives the exact algorithm behind R30's 'automatic partitioning': 'first (V % num_rows) rows get (V/num_rows + 1) NVs; first (C % num_cols) columns get (C/num_cols + 1) NVs... remainder to lower-indexed partitions'. As the judgment states, R30 is 'unverifiable without them' — these formulas define what correct automatic partitioning means.
C06--support_(defines)--R30: C06 supplies the hard capacity bound the partitioning/dispatch behavior of R30 must respect: 'maximum safe back-to-back FETCH-DISPATCH operations = number of columns (each tile BRAM holds 128 NVs... N columns x 128 = capacity)'. It defines the safety envelope within which R30's 'automatic partitioning' operates; labeling this 'defines' is slightly loose — it bounds rather than defines the rule — but the dependency is real.
C07--support_(defines)--R28: R28 mandates 'FIFOs between pipeline stages for flow control and timing isolation'; C07 defines the implementation patterns for exactly that: the 'Circular Buffer Pattern: dual-pointer (rd_ptr/wr_ptr) streaming' and 'Hierarchical Buffering: multiple BRAM levels to decouple stages'. These named patterns tell the agent how the stage-decoupling FIFOs are actually built.
C07--support_(defines)--R03: R03's 'clear module boundaries and interfaces' relies on decoupling mechanisms at those boundaries; C07's buffering patterns ('hierarchical buffering: multiple BRAM levels to decouple stages') define the standard means by which modules are kept independent across their interfaces. The link is more indirect than C07->R28 — C07 grounds modularity in general rather than any specific clause of R03.
